title: SMSF investors score surprise win
full_text:
BUDGET 2026 Self-managed super funds may become the surprise winner in the budget, with investors emerging with relatively superior tax treatment.
The Albanese government has left intact the ability of SMSF investors to borrow, despite strong lobbying from the Grattan Institute and others to close down lending facilities to the funds.
SMSF investors win under new capital gains tax rules because if you sell an asset outside super, it could now be taxed close to your marginal tax rate. But if you have it within super, the existing 33 per cent capital gains discount available after one year stays in place, creating an effective tax rate of 10 per cent.
The CGT change will work like this.
At present, you pay CGT at your marginal tax rate, which could be 47 per cent if you are on the top rate. However, if you hold the asset for more than 12 months, you are entitled to a 50 per cent discount. This treatment will now be replaced by a system that indexes CGT annually to account for inflation. It is expected to sharply raise the effective CGT tax rate.
Similarly, new negative gearing limitations are not as relevant to SMSF investors because the lower tax rates inside superannuation – 15 per cent against marginal tax rates of up to 47 per cent – means negative gearing has always been less attractive to SMSF investors in the accumulation phase.
Negative gearing will now be largely confined to new properties. Specifically, investors will no longer be able to deduct their property-related losses against their salary.
While the newly minted relative advantage of investing inside super is most obvious for property investors, the advantage may also apply to share investing, where the new CGT rules are set to weigh against “growth stocks” unless they are held for a long time. Again, this means growth stocks inside super will receive a relatively more benign tax treatment than outside super.
Despite constant growth of SMSF accounts, the sector has had little to cheer about in recent times, especially with the arrival of the Division 296 super tax, which doubled tax on earnings on amounts in super above $3m.
The new CGT rules make all investments in super relatively more attractive, a move that will benefit industry funds – key supporters of the Albanese administration. However, the distinct attraction of SMSFs is the ability of the investor to directly and actively select and manage investments in the property market.
According to the most recent Australian Taxation Office figures. among the $1 trillion in SMSF assets, around $60bn is involved with residential property.
The SMSF sector has recently had a revival in commencements as more investors moved to directly control their savings. The total number of SMSFs has grown by around 7 per cent over the past year.
However, the SMSF property sector had been clouded by speculation that the government would seek to clamp down on SMSF borrowings as part of its housing market reforms.
“SMSF lending relating to residential property is certainly not large enough to influence wider prices; we have always rejected any suggestion that it does,” SMSF Association chief executive Peter Burgess said.
Speculation over the future of the SMSF lending sector had kept the product suite available in the market limited to operators outside of the big four banks. However, the arrival of a new SMSF lending product from AMP earlier this year signalled a revival in the area. Borrowing by SMSFs is allowed only in limited circumstances through the use of limited recourse borrowing arrangements (LRBAs). Under this arrangement, if an SMSF borrower defaults, the lender can repossess the property the loan was used to acquire, but it cannot touch other assets in the SMSF.
summary: The 2026 budget maintains favourable tax treatment for self-managed super funds (SMSFs), allowing them to borrow and benefit from advantageous capital gains tax rules compared to personal investments.
author: JAMES KIRBY, ANALYSIS
heading: Superannuation and Investments
key_points: SMSF investors retain borrowing capabilities despite lobbying for restrictions.
New capital gains tax rules favour SMSFs with a 10% effective tax rate on long-held assets.
The SMSF sector has seen a 7% growth in accounts, with around $60 billion invested in residential property.
